temples.name: Sabarimala Temple
temples.description: The Sabarimala Sree Dharma Sastha Temple (Malayalam: ശബരിമല ശ്രീ ധർമ്മ ശാസ്താ ക്ഷേത്രം) (Malayalam pronunciation: [ʃabəɾimala]) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Sree Dharma Sastha where the deity is worshipped as Lord  Ayyappan, the son of the deities Shiva and Mohini (female avatar of the god Vishnu), and is situated atop the Sabarimala hill in Pathanamthitta district of Kerala, India. The temple is surrounded by 18 hills in the Periyar Tiger Reserve. It is one of the largest annual pilgrimage sites in the world, with an estimate of over 10 to 15 million devotees visiting every year.

temples.name: Tulja Bhavani Temple
temples.description: Shree Tulaja Bhavani Temple (Marathi: श्री तुळजाभवानी मंदिर), is a 12th century CE Hindu temple dedicated to goddess Bhavani. It was built in 12th century CE by Mahamandaleshwara Māradadeva of the Kadamb dynasty. Considered as one of the 51 Shakti Pithas, it is located on the banks of Mandakini River and Bori Dam in Yamunachala Hill of Balaghat Range of Tuljapur, which is 45 km northeast of Solapur, in Dharashiv district of Maharashtra in India.

temples.name: Bhawani Temple (Chandranath Peetha)
temples.description: Rising dramatically above Sitakunda's plains, Chandranath Temple crowns a forested hilltop 315 metres above sea level in southeastern Bangladesh. It is doubly sacred — a Shakti Peetha where the right arm of Goddess Sati fell, and a Shaiva seat where Shiva is worshipped as Chandranath. References appear in the medieval text Nigamkalpataru, and the Tripura kingdom endowed the temple nearly 800 years ago. Every spring, over a million pilgrims ascend its stone steps for the Shiva Chaturdashi fair, one of Bangladesh's largest Hindu gatherings.
temples.shortDescription: A hilltop Shakti Peetha in Sitakunda, Bangladesh — doubly sacred as the spot where Sati's right arm fell and as a revered Shaiva seat of Chandranath.

festivals.name: Shiva Chaturdashi Fair
festivals.description: A 10-day pilgrimage festival drawing over a million devotees to climb the hill; one of the largest Hindu gatherings in Bangladesh, culminating on Dol Purnima.

temples.name: Srisailam Bhramaramba Temple
temples.description: Deep in the Nallamala forest on the banks of the Krishna River, Bhramaramba — 'Mother of Bees' — is enshrined as one of the 18 Maha Shakti Peethas, where Sati's throat is believed to have fallen. This fierce form of Parvati takes her name from the legend in which she manifested as a swarm of black bees to slay the demon Arunasura. Sharing a walled complex with the Mallikarjuna Jyotirlinga, Srisailam is uniquely the only site in India that is simultaneously a Jyotirlinga and an 18 Maha Shakti Peetha.
temples.shortDescription: A forest Shakti Peetha where Parvati as Bhramaramba — 'Mother of Bees' — is worshipped where Sati's throat fell; the only site in India that is both a Jyotirlinga and a Maha Shakti Peetha.

temples.name: Ajanta Caves
temples.description: The Ajanta Caves are 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments dating from the second century BCE to about 480 CE in Aurangabad district of Maharashtra state in India. Ajanta Caves are a  UNESCO World Heritage Site. Universally regarded as masterpieces of Buddhist religious art, the caves include paintings and rock-cut sculptures, described as among the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian art, particularly expressive paintings that present emotions through gesture, pose and form.

temples.name: Chamunda Devi Temple
temples.description:
Shri Chamunda Devi Temple also known as Chamunda Nandikeshwar Dham is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shri Chamunda Devi, a form of Goddess Durga, located at 19 km away from Palampur town in Dharamshala Tehsil of Kangra district of the Northern Indian state of Himachal Pradesh. This is one of the most prominent temples in Himachal Pradesh and one of the most popular all over India. It is believed that whatever vow is prayed here manifests in reality. 
The much older Aadi Himani Chamunda which is also the original shrine, is situated at the hilltop, making it difficult for pilgrims to reach.

temples.name: Chintamani Temple
temples.description:
The Chintamani Temple of Theur is a Hindu temple dedicated to Supreme God Ganesha according to Ganapatya Sect located 25 km (16 mi) from Pune, the temple is "one of the larger and more famous" of the Ashtavinayaka, the eight revered shrines of Ganesha in the Indian state of Maharashtra.
The temple lore describes how bhagwan Ganesh retrieved the wish-giving jewel Chintamani for his devotee, the sage Kapila from the greedy king Gana and how he pacifies the uneasy mind of the god Brahma, who meditated on him in Theur.

temples.name: Gangotri Temple
temples.description: Gangotri is a town and a Nagar Panchayat (municipality) in Uttarkashi district in the state of Uttarakhand, India. It is 99 km from Uttarkashi, the main district headquarter. It is a Hindu pilgrim town on the banks of the river Bhagirathi – the origin of the river Ganges. The town is located on the Greater Himalayan Range, at a height of 3,100 metres (10,200 ft). According to a popular Hindu legend, the goddess Ganga descended here when Shiva released the mighty river from the locks of his hair.

temples.name: Guhyeshwari Temple
temples.description: On the sacred southern bank of the Bagmati River, Guhyeshwari enshrines Parvati in her most esoteric form — Guhyeshwari, 'the secret goddess.' Revered as one of the 51 Shakti Peethas, tradition holds that the knees of Sati fell here. Built in the Lichhavi era and renovated by King Pratap Malla in 1653, the temple is a centre of Tantric worship; its inner sanctum is restricted to Hindus, deepening its air of mystery. The Devi Bhagavata Purana venerates this site as Guhyakali, Nepal's own face of the divine feminine.
temples.shortDescription: A Tantric Shakti Peetha on the Bagmati River where Parvati is worshipped in her secret form, Guhyeshwari — believed to mark where Sati's knees fell.

temples.name: Anjeyanadri Hanuman Temple
temples.description: Anjeyanadri Hill (Anjaneyadri Betta) in Hanumanahalli near Hampi, Karnataka, is revered as the birthplace of the Hindu deity Hanuman, who was born here to Anjana and is thus called Anjaneya — 'son of Anjana'. The hilltop Hanuman temple, reached by approximately 575 steps, houses a rock-carved idol of Lord Hanuman along with shrines of Rama, Sita, and Anjana in the surrounding area. The hill stands within the ancient Kishkinda region of the Ramayana and overlooks the spectacular Hampi landscape and the Tungabhadra River below.
temples.shortDescription: Hilltop temple on the hill traditionally identified as Hanuman's birthplace, with 575 steps and a rock-carved idol, overlooking Hampi.

temples.name: Yantrodharaka Hanuman Temple
temples.description: The Yantrodharaka Hanuman Temple (also known as Pranadeva Temple) stands atop Malyavana Hill on the banks of the Tungabhadra River in Hampi, Karnataka, and is traditionally identified as the spot where Rama and Hanuman first met. The shrine was eulogised by Vyasaraya, the great 15th-century Madhva saint and poet, who installed the yantra (sacred diagram) beneath the idol — giving the temple its name, 'Yantrodharaka' meaning 'Bearer of the Yantra'. A Kodandarama Temple stands adjacent, and the hilltop location offers sweeping views over the UNESCO World Heritage landscape of Hampi.
temples.shortDescription: 15th-century hilltop Hanuman temple at the legendary spot where Rama and Hanuman first met, eulogised by the Madhva saint Vyasaraya.

Acharya Kishore Kunal is the secretary of the Mahavir Mandir Temple Trust, Patna. After the Vaishno Devi Temple, this temple has the highest earnings of over 1 lakh per day rendering its trust budgets as the second highest in the northern part of the country. The temple attracts huge crowds on festivals like Ramnavami. It is one of the leading temples dedicated to the worship of Lord Hanuman.

temples.name: Mehandipur Balaji Temple
temples.description:
Mehandipur Balaji Temple  is a Hindu temple located in Mehandipur, Dausa district, in the Indian state of Rajasthan. The temple is dedicated to the deity Hanuman. The name Balaji refers to the childhood form of Hanuman, revered in several parts of the country. Unlike similar religious sites, it is located in a town rather than the countryside. Its reputation for ritualistic healing and exorcism of evil spirits attracts many pilgrims from Rajasthan and elsewhere.
The temple is famous worldwide.

It is one of the 51 Shakti Peethas in Shaktism denomination of Hinduism. It is one of the two Shakti Peethas in Pakistan, the other one being Sharada Peeth in the Neelum Valley of Azad Jammu and Kashmir. It is a form of Durga or Devi in a mountain cavern on the banks of the Hingol River.

temples.description: Suryanar Kovil (also called Suryanar Temple) is a Hindu temple dedicated to the deity Hindu Sun-God, Surya, located in near a small town Aduthurai, Thanjavur District in Tamil Nadu, India. The presiding deity is Suriyanar, the Sun and his consorts Ushadevi and Pratyusha Devi. The temple also has separate shrines for the other eight planetary deities. The temple is considered one of the nine Navagraha temples in Tamil Nadu. The temple is one of the few historic temples dedicated to Sun god and is also the only temple in Tamil Nadu which has shrines for all the planetary deities.

temples.name: Jagannath Temple
temples.description: Built in the Kalinga style by Anantavarman Chodaganga of the Eastern Ganga dynasty in the 12th century, the Jagannath Temple in Puri is among India's most revered shrines — and one of the four Char Dham. Lord Jagannath, along with siblings Balabhadra and Subhadra, is enshrined on the jewelled Ratnabedi platform as deities carved from sacred neem wood. Entry is restricted to Hindus. The temple's Rath Yatra — when the three deities ride massive wooden chariots through Puri's Grand Road — is one of the largest annual religious gatherings on earth.
temples.shortDescription: A 12th-century Kalinga-style Char Dham temple in Puri housing Lord Jagannath, famed for the annual Rath Yatra chariot festival — one of the world's largest religious processions.

temples.description: The Delwada Temples or Delvada Temples  are a group of Śvētāmbara Jain temples located about 2+1⁄2 kilometres from the Mount Abu settlement in Sirohi District, Rajasthan's only hill station. The earliest were built by Vimal Shah, a Jain minister of Solanki king of Gurjaratra, Bhima I and  additions to the temples were made by Vastupala, Jain minister of Vaghelas of Gurjaratra.  They date between the 11th and 16th centuries, forming some of the most famous monuments in the style of Solanki architecture, famous for their use of a very pure white marble and intricate marble carvings.

temples.name: Kalighat Kali Temple
temples.description:
Kalighat Kali Temple is a Hindu temple in Kalighat, Kolkata, West Bengal, India, dedicated to the Hindu goddess Kali, one of the 10 Mahavidyas in the Hindu tantric tradition and the supreme deity in the Kalikula worship tradition. The temple is one of the 51 Shakti Pithas in India.
According to the Devi Bhagavata Purana, Kalika Purana and Shakti Peetha Stotram, the toes of the right foot of Goddess Sati fell here, after Lord Vishnu's Sudarshan Chakra splintered her body into many parts to calm down Mahadev's rage during his cosmic dance.

temples.name: Dakshineswar Kali Temple
temples.description: Dakshineswar Kali Temple or Dakshineswar Kalibari is a Hindu navaratna style temple in Dakshineswar, Kolkata, West Bengal, India, on the eastern bank of the Hooghly River. The presiding deity of the temple is Bhavatarini (Kali), a form of Mahadevi or Parashakti Adya Kali, otherwise known as Adishakti Kalika. The temple was built in 1855 by Rani Rashmoni, a zamindar (feudal lord), and a devotee of Kali. The temple is associated with avatar Sri Ramakrishna and Sarada Devi, his wife and devotee mystic, both of 19th century Bengal.

Founded in the year 1817, the temple shrines the Hindu goddess of power and strength. Bhadrakali or Durga covers an area of 135 ropanies. There are two ways to reach atop: the eastern way has 292 steps to reach the temple while traversing from the southern part is only 265 steps. In the year 1817, the temple was previously known as “Mudule Thumpko.

temples.name: Kamakhya Temple
temples.description: Kamakhya Temple (Rajbanshi: Kāmākhyā moṇḍir; Assamese: kamakhya mondir) is a Hindu temple at Nilachal hills in Guwahati, Assam is one of the oldest and most revered centres of Tantric practices, dedicated to the goddess Kamakhya. The temple is the center of the Kulachara Tantra Marga and the site of the Ambubachi Mela, an annual festival that celebrates the menstruation of the goddess. Structurally, the temple is dated to the 8th-9th century with many subsequent rebuildings—and the final hybrid architecture defines a local style called Nilachal.

The temple is located at Dwarka city of Gujarat, India, which is one of the destinations of Char Dham, a Hindu pilgrimage circuit. The main shrine of the five-storied building, supported by 72 pillars, is known as Jagat Mandir or Nija Mandir. Archaeological findings suggest the original temple was built in 200 BCE at the earliest.

temples.name: Bankey Bihari Temple
temples.description:
Banke Bihari Temple is a Hindu temple situated in the town of Vrindavan, Mathura district of Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is dedicated to Banke Bihari who is believed to be the combined form of Radha and Krishna. Banke Bihari was originally worshipped at Nidhivan, Vrindavan. Later, when Banke Bihari temple was constructed around 1864, the icon of Banke Bihari was moved to its present temple.
In the Banke Bihari temple, the icon of Radha Krishna's united form stands in the Tribhanga posture.

temples.name: Temple of the Vedic Planetarium
temples.description: Rising 340 feet above the sacred floodplains where the Ganges meets the Jalangi, the Temple of the Vedic Planetarium is unlike any structure attempted in the modern era. Its Capitol-inspired dome shelters a cosmological universe drawn from the Srimad Bhagavatam — a spiralling Vedic cosmos with Mount Meru at its centre. Conceived by Srila Prabhupada in 1976 and built upon land identified as the birthplace of Sri Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, it is under construction since 2009. During Gaura Purnima, hundreds of thousands of devotees from every continent circumambulate in night-long sankirtan.
temples.shortDescription: The world's largest temple under construction — a Vedic cosmological wonder rising 340 ft above Sri Chaitanya's sacred birthplace.

temples.name: ISKCON Temple Pune
temples.description: Nestled in the hills of Kondhwa on Pune's southern edge, the New Vedic Cultural Center is far more than a place of worship. Three golden altars blaze with the gold-plated forms of Sri Sri Radha Vrindavanchandra, Sri Sri Gaura-Nitai, and Sri Sri Jagannath-Baldev-Subhadra, their brilliance amplified by cascading chandeliers and ceiling murals depicting Krishna's eternal pastimes. Vedic aesthetics drawn from Jagannath Puri and Tirupati converge here, inspiring awe and inner stillness. The ceaseless sound of kirtana fills the marble halls, drawing thousands of seekers each day.
temples.shortDescription: Pune's grandest ISKCON temple — three gold-plated altars, Vedic murals and ceaseless kirtan draw thousands of Krishna devotees daily.

temples.name: Mangalagauri Temple
temples.description: Perched atop Mangalagauri Hill in the ancient pilgrimage city of Gaya, this Maha Shakti Peetha marks where the breast of Goddess Sati fell — a symbol of divine nourishment and cosmic motherhood. The hillside sanctum is deliberately intimate; only two to five worshippers may enter at a time, creating an atmosphere of intense personal devotion. An Akhand Deep burns ceaselessly within, and subsidiary shrines to Kali, Ganesha, Hanuman and Shiva surround the main goddess.
temples.shortDescription: A Maha Shakti Peetha atop Mangalagauri Hill in Gaya, marking where Sati's breast fell — worshipped as the nourishing form of the Divine Mother.

temples.name: Maya Devi Temple
temples.description:
Maya Devi Temple, Haridwar (Hindi: माया देवी मंदिर, हरिद्वार) is a Hindu temple dedicated to goddess Maya in the holy city of Haridwar of Uttarakhand state in India. It is believed that the heart and navel of goddess Sati fell in the region where the temple stands today and thus it is sometimes referred to as a Shakta pitha.
Goddess Maya is the Adhisthatri deity of Haridwar. She is a three-headed and four-armed deity who is believed to be an incarnation of Shakti. Haridwar was previously known as Mayapuri in reverence to this deity.

temples.name: Patan Devi Temple
temples.description: Patan Devi is credited with giving Patna its very name, and stands as one of the city's oldest shrines. Two temples — Badi (Big) and Chhoti (Small) Patan Devi — form a dual Shakti Peetha complex where the right thigh of Goddess Sati is believed to have fallen. Images were reportedly installed by Man Singh, general of Emperor Akbar, lending the site a rare confluence of Brahmanical and Mughal heritage. Each morning and evening priests bathe the goddess, offer prasad and perform aarti to ringing bells.
temples.shortDescription: A twin Shakti Peetha complex in Patna that gave the city its name, marking where Sati's right thigh fell — one of Bihar's most ancient goddess shrines.

temples.name: ISKCON Temple Vrindavan
temples.description: In the sacred dust of Vrindavan — where Krishna himself once played — stands Srila Prabhupada's masterwork: a gleaming white marble temple inaugurated on Ram Navami 1975 and now one of the holiest Vaishnava destinations in the world. Three altars house Krishna-Balaram, Radha-Shyamasundar with the gopis, and Gaura-Nitai. Pilgrims descend into a sunken courtyard shaded by an ancient tamal tree — believed to be the very species Krishna would rest beneath — and experience a timeless intimacy with the Divine that the chaos of modern Vrindavan cannot penetrate.
temples.shortDescription: Prabhupada's 1975 white marble masterpiece in Vrindavan — three sacred altars where Krishna and Balaram are worshipped together.

temples.name: Ram Janmabhoomi Temple
temples.description: Ram Mandir stands at the heart of one of India's most consequential religious disputes — the Ram Janmabhoomi conflict — resolved by the Supreme Court in 2019. Designed in the Nagara style by architect Chandrakant Sompura, the three-storeyed pink sandstone structure soars 161 feet and was consecrated on 22 January 2024 in a ceremony presided over by the Prime Minister. The presiding deity is Ram Lalla — the infant form of Rama — installed at his mythological birthplace.
temples.shortDescription: A monumental Nagara-style temple consecrated in January 2024, built on the site revered as the birthplace of Lord Ram after a landmark Supreme Court verdict.

temples.name: Bharat Mandir, Nandigram
temples.description: Nandigram, known by its ancient name Bharatkund, is a sacred village in the Sohawal tehsil of Ayodhya district, Uttar Pradesh, approximately 19 km south of Ayodhya city. It is revered in the Ramayana tradition as the place where Prince Bharata, refusing to rule from the capital in his brother Rama's absence, placed Rama's padukas (sandals) on the throne and administered the kingdom in symbolic regency from this village. The Bharat Mandir and the sacred Bharat Sarovar (kund) here draw pilgrims from across India who honour Bharata's legendary devotion and renunciation.
temples.shortDescription: Sacred village where Prince Bharata governed during Rama's exile, enshrining Rama's padukas on the throne as an act of unwavering devotion.

temples.name: Kanak Bhawan
temples.description: Kanak Bhawan (lit. 'Golden Palace') is a temple in Ram Janmabhoomi, Ayodhya, which is in the north-east of Ramkot. It is one of the most famous temples of Ayodhya. It is believed that this palace (temple) was gifted by Kaikeyi, immediately after the marriage of Rama with Sita and hence, it is their personal palace. According to the mythology, after the original Kanak Bhawan was damaged, it was rebuilt by Krishna himself in the Dvapar Yuga. It is believed that it was renovated by Vikramaditya in the medieval period.

temples.name: Rukmini Devi Temple
temples.description:
The Rukmini Devi Temple is a temple in Dwarka, 2 kilometres (1.2 mi) away from Dwarka, Gujarat, India. It is dedicated to Goddess Rukmini (Lord Krishna's chief queen, beloved consort and the incarnation of Devi Mahalakshmi in Dvapara Yuga).  The temple is believed to date back to the 5th century BC, though its present form is thought to originate from the 12th century.
It is a richly carved temple decorated with sculptures of gods and goddesses on the exterior with the sanctum housing the main image of Rukmini.

temples.name: Shirdi Sai Baba Temple
temples.description: The Shri Saibaba Samadhi Mandir is built over the sacred resting place of Sai Baba of Shirdi, a saint venerated by Hindus, Muslims and Zoroastrians alike. Originally the Buti Wada — a stone mansion built by Nagpur millionaire Gopalrao Buti between 1915 and 1918 — it became Sai Baba's samadhi on 15 October 1918, his Mahasamadhi day. The marble shrine now draws an estimated 60,000 pilgrims daily, rising to over 200,000 during major festivals.
temples.shortDescription: Built over Sai Baba's samadhi, Shirdi's marble-and-gold shrine draws 60,000 pilgrims daily and is one of India's most visited pilgrimage sites.

temples.name: Kanchi Kamakoti Peetham
temples.description: Nestled in the ancient temple city of Kanchipuram, the Sri Kanchi Kamakoti Peetham is one of Hinduism's most venerable Advaita Vedanta institutions, traditionally ascribed to Adi Shankaracharya. For centuries it has been a living centre of Vedic learning and Sanskrit scholarship, its spiritual identity woven intimately with the goddess Kamakshi. The Peetham rose to global prominence under its 68th Shankaracharya, Sri Chandrashekharendra Saraswati, who is widely revered as a saint.
temples.shortDescription: One of Hinduism's most venerable Advaita Vedanta institutions in Kanchipuram, traditionally founded by Adi Shankaracharya and inseparable from the goddess Kamakshi.

temples.name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
temples.description:
Kashi Vishwanath Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva. It is located in Vishwanath Gali, in Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh, India. The temple is a Hindu pilgrimage site and is one of the twelve Jyotirlinga shrines. The presiding deity is known by the names Vishwanath and Vishweshwara (IAST: Viśvanātha and Viśveśvara), meaning Lord of the Universe.
The original temple, called the Adi Vishveshwar Temple, was demolished by Mohammad of Ghor during his invasion of India. Subsequently, the template was rebuilt by Man Singh I and Todar Mal under the emperor Akbar.

temples.name: Kedarnath Temple
temples.description:
Kēdāranātha Temple (Sanskrit: केदारनाथ मंदिर, IAST: Kēdāranātha Mandira, lit. 'temple of the God of the field') is a Hindu temple, one of the twelve jyotirlinga of Śiva. The temple is located on the Garhwal Himalayan range 
near the Mandākinī river, in the state of Uttarakhand, India. Due to extreme weather conditions, the temple is open to the general public only between the months of April (Akṣaya Tritiya) and November (Kārtika Pūrṇimā, the autumn full moon). During the winters, the vigraha (deity) of the temple is carried down to Ukhimath to be worshiped for the next six months.

Kedarnath, a Hindu holy city, is located 86 km from Rudraprayag. The man-eating leopard of Rudraprayag hunted and written about by Jim Corbett lived here.

temples.name: Kailasa Temple
temples.description:
The Kailasha (IAST: Kailāśa) or Kailasha-natha (IAST: Kailāśa-nātha) temple is the largest of the rock-cut Hindu temples at the Ellora Caves in Sambhaji Nagar district of Maharashtra, India.
A megalith carved into a cliff face, it is considered one of the most remarkable cave temples in the world because of its size, architecture, and sculptural treatment. It has been called "the climax of the rock-cut phase of Indian architecture". The top of the structure over the sanctuary is 32.6 metres (107 ft) above the level of the court below.

temples.name: Elephanta Caves
temples.description: The Elephanta Caves form a collection of cave temples predominantly dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva; UNESCO has designated them as a World Heritage Site. They are located on Elephanta Island, or Gharapuri (literally meaning "the city of the caves"), in Mumbai Harbour, 10 kilometres (6.2 mi) east of Mumbai in the Indian state of Maharashtra. The island, about 2 kilometres (1.2 mi) west of the Jawaharlal Nehru Port, features five Hindu caves, a few Buddhist stupa mounds that date back to the 2nd century BCE, and two Buddhist caves with water tanks.

temples.name: Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram
temples.description:
The Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram is a collection of 7th- and 8th-century CE religious monuments in the coastal resort town of Mahabalipuram, Tamil Nadu, India and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, about 60 kilometres (37 mi) south of Chennai.
The site has 40 ancient monuments and Hindu temples, including one of the largest open-air rock reliefs in the world: the Descent of the Ganges or Arjuna's Penance.

temples.name: Lingaraja Temple
temples.description:
Lingaraja Temple (Odia: [liŋɡɔraːd͡ʒɔ] ) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva and is one of the oldest temples in Bhubaneswar, the capital of the Indian state of Odisha, India. The temple is the most prominent landmark of Bhubaneswar city and one of the major tourist attractions of the state. Shiva's consort and the temple's presiding Goddess, Parvati, is referred to as Annapurna or Girija.
The Lingaraja temple is the largest temple in Bhubaneswar. The central tower of the temple is 180 ft (55 m) tall.

temples.name: Hoysaleswara Temple
temples.description: Hoysaleshwara temple (also spelled as Hoysaleswara, Hoysaleśvara or Hoywalesvara temple), also referred simply as the Halebidu temple, is a 12th-century Hindu temple dedicated to the god Shiva. It is the largest monument in Halebidu, a town in the state of Karnataka, India, and the former capital of the Hoysala Empire. The temple was built on the banks of a large man-made lake, and sponsored by King Vishnuvardhana of the Hoysala Empire. Its construction started around 1121 CE and was complete in 1160 CE.

temples.name: Gangaikonda Cholapuram Temple
temples.description: The Brihadisvara Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva in Gangaikonda Cholapuram, Jayankondam, in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu. Completed in 1035 CE by Rajendra Chola I as a part of his new capital, this Chola dynasty era temple is similar in design, and has a similar name, as the older 11th century, Brihadeeswarar Temple about 70 kilometres (43 mi) to the southwest in Thanjavur. The Gangaikonda Cholapuram Temple is smaller yet more refined than the Thanjavur Temple. Both are among the largest Shiva temples in South India and examples of Dravidian style temples.

temples.name: Airavatesvara Temple
temples.description:
Airavatesvara Temple is a Hindu temple of Chola architecture located in Darasuram, a suburb of Kumbakonam, Thanjavur District in the South Indian state of Tamil Nadu. This temple, built by Chola emperor Rajaraja II in the 12th century CE is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, along with the Brihadeeswara Temple at Thanjavur, the Gangaikondacholisvaram Temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram that are referred to as the Great Living Chola Temples.
The Airavatesvarar temple is one among a cluster of eighteen medieval era large Hindu temples in the Kumbakonam area, Thanjavur District.

temples.name: Kailasanathar Temple
temples.description: The Kailasanathar Temple, Kanchipuram, also referred to as the Kailasanatha temple, is a Pallava-era historic Hindu temple in Kanchipuram, Tamil Nadu, India. Dedicated to Shiva, it is one of the oldest surviving monuments in Kanchipuram. It reflects a Dravidian architecture and was built around 700 CE by Narasimhavarman II with additions by Mahendravarman III. A square-plan temple, it has a mukha-mandapa (entrance hall), a maha-mandapa (gathering hall) and a primary garbha-griya (sanctum) topped with a four-storey vimana.

temples.description: Kedareshwara Temple (also spelt "Kedaresvara" or "Kedareshvara") is a Hoysala era construction in the historically important town of Halebidu, in the Hassan district of Karnataka state, India. It is located a short distance away from the famous Hoysaleswara Temple. The temple was constructed by Hoysala King Veera Ballala II (r. 1173–1220 CE) and his Queen Ketaladevi, and the main deity is Ishwara (another name for the Hindu god Shiva). The temple is protected as a monument of national importance by the Archaeological Survey of India.

It is considered as one of the best examples of temples preserved from the medieval period in India. Because of its outstanding preservation and testimony to the Chandela culture, the temple was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.

temples.name: Devi Jagadambi Temple
temples.description:
Devi Jagadambika Temple or Jagadambika Temple is one of a group of about 25 temples at Khajuraho, Madhya Pradesh, India. Along with the other temples at Khajuraho, the temple was listed as a World Heritage Site because of its outstanding architecture, art, and historical importance. The temples of Khajuraho were built by the rulers of the Chandela dynasty between the 10th and the 12th centuries.
Devi Jagadambika temple, in a group to the north, is one of the most finely decorated temples at Khajuraho. It is named after Jagadambika, a Hindu goddess related to devi.

temples.name: Annamalaiyar Temple
temples.description: At the foot of the ancient red hill of Arunachala — believed by devotees to be Shiva himself made manifest on earth — this temple enshrines the Agni Lingam, the fire element among the Pancha Bhuta Sthalas. Legend holds that Shiva appeared here as an endless column of light blazing across all three worlds. Each year, millions converge for Karthigai Deepam, when a beacon of ghee and camphor is lit atop the hill as the full moon rises. Sages through the ages, most famously Ramana Maharshi, have declared Arunachala not merely sacred but Shiva's living form — a hill that is itself the guru.
temples.shortDescription: Pancha Bhuta Sthala enshrining Shiva as the Agni Lingam (fire element) at the sacred Arunachala hill; famed for Karthigai Deepam beacon festival.

temples.name: Mundeshwari Temple
temples.description:
The Mundeshwari Devi Temple (IAST: Muṇḍeśvarī) is a Hindu temple, located at Ramgarh Village, 608 feet (185 m) on the Mundeshwari Hills of Kaimur plateau near Son River, in the Bhojpuri region of Indian state of Bihar. It is an Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) protected monument since 1915.
It is an ancient temple which is believed to be dedicated to the worship of the goddess Durga and god Shiva, and is claimed as the oldest functional Hindu temple in the world.

temples.name: Amarnath Cave Temple
temples.description: At 3,888 m in the Himalayas, Amarnath is a cave shrine where meltwater dripping from a 40-metre limestone roof freezes into a natural ice stalagmite worshipped as the Swayambhu Shiva lingam. The lingam waxes through summer as Himalayan snow melts, then recedes — nearby formations are identified as Parvati and Ganesha. Mentioned in the 6th-century Nilamata Purana, the cave is accessible only during the Shravani Mela (late June to mid-August) and draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims each year.
temples.shortDescription: A Himalayan cave shrine at 3,888 m where a naturally forming ice stalagmite is worshipped as the Swayambhu Shiva lingam, open only during the summer Yatra season.

temples.name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
temples.description:
Meenakshi Temple, also known as Meenakshi Sundareswarar Temple, is a historic Hindu temple located on the southern bank of the Vaigai River in Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. It is dedicated to Meenakshi, a form of Parvati, and her consort Sundareswarar (Shiva). The temple is theologically significant as it represents a confluence of various denominations of Hinduism such as Shaivism, Shaktism, and Vaishnavism.
While the Sangam literature mentions the temple city of Madurai, the existence of a temple is first referenced in the Tamil texts from 6th century CE.

temples.description:
Jambukeswarar Temple, Thiruvanaikaval (also Thiruvanaikal, Jambukeswaram) is a temple of Shiva in Tiruchirapalli district, in the state of Tamil Nadu, India. It is one of the five major Shiva Temples of Tamil Nadu representing the Mahābhūta or five elements; this temple represents the element of water, or neer in Tamil. The sanctum of Jambukeswara has an underground stream.In the temple, Shiva is worshipped as Jambukeshwarar and his consort Parvati as Akilandeswari.
It is one of the 275 Paadal Petra Sthalams and has inscriptions from the Chola period.

temples.name: Group of Monuments at Pattadakal
temples.description: Pattadakal stands as the crowning achievement of early medieval Indian temple-building — the place where rival schools of architecture met and merged. Two Chalukya queens commissioned its greatest temples to immortalise their husbands' military triumphs, turning victory into stone. UNESCO hailed it as 'the apogee of an eclectic art,' a living laboratory where northern Nagara towers and southern Dravidian vimanas were built side by side. The Virupaksha temple, modelled on the Kailasanatha at Kanchipuram, still receives active worship — a continuum from 8th-century queens to pilgrims today.
temples.shortDescription: UNESCO World Heritage complex of 7th–8th century Chalukya temples blending Nagara and Dravidian styles, Karnataka.

temples.name: Badami Cave Temples
temples.description: The Badami cave temples are a complex of Buddhist, Hindu and Jain cave temples located in Badami, a town in the Bagalkot district in northern part of Karnataka, India. The caves are important examples of Indian rock-cut architecture, especially Badami Chalukya architecture, and the earliest date from the 6th century. Badami is a modern name and was previously known as "Vataapi", the capital of the early Chalukya dynasty, which ruled much of Karnataka from the 6th to the 8th century.

temples.name: Shore Temple
temples.description:
The Shore Temple (c. 725 AD) is a complex of temples and shrines that overlooks the shore of the Bay of Bengal. It is located in Mahabalipuram, about 60 kilometres (37 mi) south of Chennai in Tamil Nadu, India.
It is a structural temple, built with blocks of granite, dating from the 8th century AD. At the time of its creation, the site was a busy port during the reign of Narasimhavarman II of the Indian Pallava dynasty. As one of the Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram, it has been classified as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1984.

temples.name: Pancha Rathas
temples.description: Pancha Rathas (also known as Five Rathas or Pandava Rathas or Ainthinai kovil) is a monument complex at Mahabalipuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, in the Chengalpattu district of the state of Tamil Nadu, India. Pancha Rathas is an example of monolithic Indian rock-cut architecture. The complex was initially thought to have carved during the reign of King Narasimhavarman I (630–668 CE). However, historians such as Nagaswamy attributed all of monuments in Mahabalipuram to Narasimhavarman II (c. 690–725 CE) with the discovery of new inscriptions.

temples.name: Khajuraho Group of Temples
temples.description: Rising from the plains of Madhya Pradesh, Khajuraho is one of the world's great artistic enigmas — a medieval city where the sacred and sensual are inseparable. Built by the Chandela dynasty between the 9th and 11th centuries, the temples once numbered 85; roughly 25 survive today, their towering shikhara spires still intact. Their outer walls teem with thousands of carvings including the famous erotic scenes scholars read as tantric initiations — desire as a threshold on the journey inward. Inside each sanctuary the imagery gives way to stillness. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986.
temples.shortDescription: UNESCO-listed Chandela temples in Madhya Pradesh, famed for exquisite erotic and devotional stone carvings across towering Nagara shikharas.

temples.name: Ellora Caves
temples.description: The Ellora Caves are a UNESCO World Heritage Site in Aurangabad, India. It is one of the largest rock-cut  cave complexes in the world, with artwork dating from AD 600–1000, including Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain caves. The complex is a leading example of Indian rock-cut architecture, and several are not strictly "caves" in that they have no roof.  Cave 16 features the largest single monolithic rock excavation in the world, the Kailash temple, a chariot-shaped monument dedicated to the god Shiva.

temples.name: Janaki Sthan, Sitamarhi
temples.description: Sitamarhi is revered as the birthplace of goddess Sita — the place where King Janaka found the infant Sita while ploughing a field, as told in the Valmiki Ramayana. The city's very name means 'Sita's dwelling', and Janaki Sthan marks the exact spot, drawing pilgrims for the annual Janaki Vivah ceremony that re-enacts the marriage of Rama and Sita.

temples.name: Konark Sun Temple
temples.description: Konark Sun Temple is a 13th-century CE Hindu Sun temple at Konark about 35 kilometres (22 mi) northeast from Puri city on the coastline in Puri district, Odisha, India. The temple is attributed to king Narasingha Deva I of the Eastern Ganga dynasty about 1250 CE. Dedicated to the Hindu Sun-god Surya, it reflects the pinnacle of kalingan architecture and artistic excellence, what remains of the temple complex has the appearance of a 100-foot (30 m) high chariot with immense wheels and horses, all carved from stone.

temples.name: Modhera Sun Temple
temples.description: The  Sun Temple of Modhera is a Hindu temple dedicated to the solar deity Surya located in the village of Modhera in Mehsana district, Gujarat, India. The temple is situated on the bank of the river Pushpavati, and was constructed after 1026-1027 CE during the reign of Bhima I of the Chaulukya dynasty. The temple is no longer used for worship and is a protected monument maintained by the Archaeological Survey of India. The temple complex has three components: Gūḍhamanḍapa, the shrine hall; Sabhamanḍapa, the assembly hall and Kunḍa, the reservoir.

temples.name: Swaminarayan Akshardham
temples.description: Built over 13 years without a single piece of steel or iron, Swaminarayan Akshardham rises from 6,000 tonnes of intricately carved pink Rajasthani sandstone. The central mandir stands 108 ft tall with 17 domes, 97 carved pillars and 264 sculpted figures, all created by BAPS artisans under Pramukh Swami Maharaj and inaugurated in 1992. Spread across 23 acres, the complex combines a traditional mandir with cultural exhibitions, a water show and gardens.
temples.shortDescription: A monumental 1992 BAPS temple in Gandhinagar built entirely from carved pink sandstone — no steel or iron — standing 108 ft tall with 17 domes and 264 sculpted figures.

temples.name: Tripura Sundari Temple
temples.description: Tripura Sundari Temple is a Hindu temple of the Goddess Tripura Sundari, better known locally as Devi Tripureshwari the third mahavidya and main form of Parvati. The temple is situated in the ancient city of Udaipur, about 55 km from Agartala, Tripura and can be reached by train and road from Agartala. It is believed to be one of the holiest Hindu shrines in this part of the country and witnesses the highest number of visitors for a temple in North-East India, after Kamakhya Temple in Assam. The state of Tripura is named after this temple.

temples.name: Ugratara Temple
temples.description: Built by Ahom King Siva Singh in 1725 AD on the western bank of the sacred Jor Pukhuri tank, Ugratara Devalaya is one of Guwahati's foremost Shakti Peethas — believed to mark where Sati's navel fell. Uniquely, the sanctum holds no idol; a small water-filled pit is worshipped as the fierce goddess Tara. Designed in the Nilachala style with a towering shikhara, the temple sustained damage in the 1897 Great Assam Earthquake and was subsequently restored.
temples.shortDescription: An Ahom-built Shakti Peetha in Guwahati where a water-filled pit, rather than any idol, is venerated as the fierce goddess Tara.

temples.name: Undavalli Caves
temples.description: Hewn directly from a sandstone hillside overlooking the Krishna River, the Undavalli Caves are one of the finest examples of early Indian rock-cut architecture. Originally a Jain retreat, the complex was adapted by Buddhist monks before evolving into a Hindu site under the Vishnukundina kings. The centrepiece is a four-storeyed cave temple — 29 m long — whose second floor shelters a magnificent 5-metre reclining Vishnu carved from a single block of granite. Pilastered facades and shrines to the Hindu Trinity make this a layered artistic landmark of the Deccan.
temples.shortDescription: A 4th–5th century CE multi-storeyed rock-cut temple complex near Vijayawada, housing a monumental reclining Vishnu carved from a single granite block.

temples.name: Varaha Cave Temple
temples.description: Varaha Cave Temple (i.e., Varaha Mandapa or the Adivaraha Cave) is a rock-cut cave temple located at Mamallapuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal in Kancheepuram District in Tamil Nadu, India. It is part of the hill top village, which is 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) to the north of the main Mahabalipurm sites of rathas and the Shore Temple. It is an example of Indian rock-cut architecture dating from the late 7th century. The temple is one of the finest testimonial to the ancient Hindu rock-cut cave architecture, out of many such caves also called mandapas.

temples.name: Krishna Mandapam
temples.description:
Mandapa of Krishna or Krishna Mandapam is a monument at Mahabalipuram, on the Coromandel Coast of the Bay of Bengal, in the Kancheepuram district of the state of Tamil Nadu, India. It is part of the Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram, a UNESCO World Heritage Site inscribed in 1984. It is located on a hillock next to the open rock relief of Descent of the Ganges (Mahabalipuram).
It is constituted by an originally open-air bas-relief dedicated to Krishna, dating to the mid-seventh century, which was later enclosed within a mandapa in the 16th century during the Vijayanagara Empire.

temples.name: Badrinath Temple
temples.description: Badarinath Temple, also known as Badarinarayana Temple, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu. It is located in the town of Badrinath in Chamoli district of Uttarakhand, India. The temple is one of the 108 Divya Desams, sacred to Vaishnavism, where Vishnu is worshipped as Badrinath. Due to extreme weather conditions in the Himalayas, the temple is open for six months each year, from late April to early November. It is one of the most visited pilgrimage centers of India, having recorded 2.8 million (28 lakh) visits in just 2 months in 2022. It is one of the Char Dham pilgrimage sites.

temples.name: Sree Padmanabhaswamy Temple
temples.description:
The Padmanabhaswamy Temple (Malayalam: [pɐd̪mɐnaːbʰɐswaːmi]) is a Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu in Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, India. It is one of the 108 Divya Desams, sacred sites of the Sri Vaishnava tradition.
The temple's principal deity is Anantha Padmanabhaswamy, a form of Vishnu depicted in yogic sleep, on his serpent mount named Shesha. Padmanabhaswamy is the tutelary deity of the Travancore royal family. The titular Maharaja of Travancore, Moolam Thirunal Rama Varma, is the current trustee of the temple.

The temple is dedicated to Venkateswara, a form of Vishnu, who is believed to have appeared on earth to save mankind from trials and troubles of Kali Yuga. temples.name: Srimushnam Bhu Varaha Swamy Temple
temples.description: At Srimushnam stands one of the eight swayambhu Vishnu kshetras — shrines where Vishnu is believed to have self-manifested, ungiven by human hands. This is the only shrine in that sacred group where Vishnu is venerated solely in his Varaha form — the cosmic boar who plunged into primordial waters to rescue Bhu Devi from the demon Hiranyaksha. After slaying the asura, Vishnu granted his dying wish and turned southward; so the deity's boar head still gazes south while his body welcomes devotees from the west. The sweat that fell from Varaha during that cosmic battle is said to have formed the temple tank, the Nithya Pushkarani.
temples.shortDescription: One of eight swayambhu Vishnu shrines in India; the only one enshrining Varaha the boar-avatar, at Srimushnam, Cuddalore, Tamil Nadu.

temples.name: Pushkar Varaha Temple
temples.description: Perched above the sacred Pushkar Sarovar, the Varaha temple is among the oldest and most venerated shrines in Rajasthan — a towering brick-red structure rising on a plinth of thirty steps, guarded by life-size Dwarpalas and carved pillars bearing Garuda. It enshrines Varaha, Vishnu's cosmic boar avatar who plunged into the primordial ocean to rescue the Earth goddess from the demon Hiranyaksha. The surrounding lake is Tirtha-Guru — teacher of all pilgrimage waters — and Pushkar itself is Tirtha-Raja, king of sacred sites. Bathing here on Kartik Purnima is said to sever the cycle of rebirth.
temples.shortDescription: Ancient Varaha shrine above Pushkar Lake — Rajasthan's holiest town, Tirtha-Raja (king of sacred sites), famed for the great Kartik Purnima fair.

temples.name: Group of Monuments at Hampi
temples.description:
Hampi or Hampe (Kannada: [hɐmpe]), also referred to as the Group of Monuments at Hampi, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in the town of Hampi in Vijayanagara district, east-central Karnataka, India. Hampi predates the Vijayanagara Empire; it is mentioned in the Ramayana and the Puranas of Hinduism as Pampa Devi Tirtha Kshetra. Hampi continues as a religious centre, with the Virupaksha Temple, an active Adi Shankara-linked monastery and various monuments belonging to the old city.
Hampi was the capital of the Vijayanagara Empire from 1336 to 1565 (as Vijayanagara), when it was abandoned.

temples.name: Akshardham Temple
temples.description:
Swaminarayan Akshardham is a Hindu temple and campus in Delhi, India. The temple is close to the border with Noida. Also known as Akshardham Temple or Akshardham Delhi, the complex displays traditional and modern Hindu culture and architecture. Inspired by Yogiji Maharaj and created by Pramukh Swami Maharaj, it was constructed by BAPS. It is the world's second-largest BAPS Hindu temple, following Akshardham, New Jersey, in the United States.
The temple was opened on 6 November 2005 by Pramukh Swami Maharaj in the presence of A. P. J. Abdul Kalam, Manmohan Singh, L.K Advani and B.L Joshi.

temples.name: Yamunotri Temple
temples.description:
Yamunotri Temple is a Hindu temple, situated in the western region of Garhwal Himalayas at an altitude of 3,291 metres (10,797 ft) in Uttarkashi district, Uttarakhand, India. It's just 129 km from Uttarkashi, the main district headquarters. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Yamuna, and has a black marble idol of the goddess. 
The actual temple is only accessible by a 13 kilometres (8.1 mi) trek from the town of Hanuman Chatti and a 6 kilometres (3.7 mi) walk from Janki Chatti; horses or palanquins are available for rent.

temples.name: Madhyamaheshwar Temple
temples.description: Madhyamaheshwar is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva, located in Gaundar village in Rudraprayag district, amidst the Garhwal Himalayas of Uttarakhand. Situated at an elevation of 3,497 m (11,473 ft), it is one of the Panch Kedar pilgrimage sites. The temple is believed to enshrine the navel (nabhi) of Shiva.
temples.shortDescription: Panch Kedar temple at 3,497 m enshrining the navel of Shiva.

temples.name: Vishnuprayag
temples.description: Vishnuprayag is the southernmost and first of the five Pancha Prayag confluences on the Alaknanda River in Uttarakhand, India, where the Dhauliganga meets the Alaknanda at an elevation of 1,372 m.
temples.shortDescription: Southernmost of the five Pancha Prayag confluences, where the Dhauliganga meets the Alaknanda.

temples.name: Vaitheeswaran Koil
temples.description: Vaitheeswaran Koil is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva located near Mayiladuthurai, Tamil Nadu. Shiva is worshipped as Vaidyanathar, the God of healing, and the presiding planet is Angaraka (Mars).
temples.shortDescription: Shiva temple near Mayiladuthurai where the presiding Nava Graha is Angaraka (Mars).

temples.name: Swetharanyeswarar Temple
temples.description: Swetharanyeswarar Temple is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva located in Thiruvenkadu, Mayiladuthurai district, Tamil Nadu. It is one of the nine Nava Graha temples, representing Budha (Mercury).
temples.shortDescription: Shiva temple in Thiruvenkadu representing Budha (Mercury) among the Nava Graha temples.

temples.name: Tirunageswaram Naganathar Temple
temples.description: Tirunageswaram Naganathar Temple, also known as Rahu Stalam, is a Hindu temple dedicated to Shiva located near Kumbakonam, Tamil Nadu. It is the Nava Graha temple representing Rahu.
temples.shortDescription: Shiva temple near Kumbakonam representing Rahu among the Nava Graha temples.

temples.name: Srivaikuntanathan Perumal Temple
temples.description: Srivaikuntanathan Perumal Temple is a Hindu Vishnu temple located in Srivaikuntam, Tamil Nadu. It is one of the Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temples on the banks of the Thamirabarani river, presided over by Surya (Sun).
temples.shortDescription: Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temple in Srivaikuntam, presiding graha Surya (Sun).

temples.name: Vijayasana Perumal Temple
temples.description: Vijayasana Perumal Temple, also known as ThiruVaragunamangai Perumal Temple, is a Hindu Vishnu temple located in Natham, Tamil Nadu. It is one of the Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temples, presided over by Chandra (Moon).
temples.shortDescription: Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temple in Natham, presiding graha Chandra (Moon).

temples.name: Makara Nedunkuzhai Kannan Temple

temples.shortDescription: Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temple in Thenthiruperai, presiding graha Sukran (Venus).

temples.name: Aravindalochanar Temple
temples.description: Aravindalochanar Temple is a Hindu Vishnu temple located in Tholavillimangalam, Tamil Nadu. One of the Irattai Thiruppathy (Twin Tirupatis), it is a Nava Tirupati Divya Desam temple presided over by Ketu.

temples.name: Salasar Balaji Temple
temples.description: Salasar Balaji Temple is a Hindu temple for the devotees of Hindu god Hanuman. It is located in the town of Salasar near Sujangarh, in Churu district of Rajasthan, India. The Hanuman Temple is situated right in the heart of Salasar town. Every year two big fairs are organized during the months of Chaitra (March–April) and Ashwin (September - October).
temples.shortDescription: Salasar Balaji Temple is a Hindu temple for the devotees of Hindu god Hanuman.
